revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Chinju no Mori]][[id:Chinju no Mori]][[ja:鎮守の森]][[simple:Chinju no Mori]]
{{Short description|Japanese sacred forest|qq=}} [[File:Sasayama1.JPG|thumb|300x300px|A forest of Shinto shrine surrounded by rice paddies ({{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} [[:en:Tanba-Sasayama|Tanba Sasayama City]], Kawakita, Kasuga Shrine). Similar landscapes are still widely found in rice-growing areas in Japan.]] {{Nihongo|'''Chinju-no-mori'''|鎮守の森|qq=}} are {{ill|Forest|en|Forest|qid=Q4421|lt=Forest|1=Forest}}s established and maintained in or around shrines ([[Chinjugami]]) in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, surrounding {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}, {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sando|1=Sandō}}, and places of worship.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kotera |first=Yasuhiro |date=2021-12-23 |title=Sacred Forests: The Ecological Power of Reverence |url=https://www.theearthandi.org/post/sacred-forests-the-ecological-power-of-reverence |access-date=2023-03-09 |website=The Earth & I |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=ARC - Faiths and ecology - Shinto forestry |url=http://www.arcworld.org/faiths331a.html?pageID=127 |access-date=2023-03-dd |website=www.arcworld.org|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9944 |access-date=2023-03-dd |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Chinju no Mori: The Guardian of the Forest Shrine |url=https://wisata.app/en-us/diary/chinju-no-mori-temple-of-the-guardian-of-the-forest |access-date=2023-03-dd |website=Wisata App |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Chinju-no-Mori |url=http://www.gentleearth.org/Chinju-English.htm |access-date=2023-03-dd |website=www.gentleearth.org|qq=}}</ref> == Meaning == Chinju no mori refers to the {{ill|Forest|en|Forest|qid=Q4421|lt=Forest|1=Forest}} that surrounded many {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}''<ref>{{ill|Hokora|en|Hokora|qid=Q1442984|lt=祠|1=Hokora}}を含む小さな神社で、神殿のすぐ隣まで人家などが建てられている神社は除く。</ref>.'' The character for '''mori''' is often used. In some cases, the word shrine is written and read as mori, suggesting that [[Shrine Shinto]] was derived from [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]].<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20130813050315/http://www.misogi.jp/shisetsu.html「身曾岐神社 「みそぎ神社」 建物・施設紹介]</ref> The character {{nihongo|''mori''|杜|qq=}} is often used in the names of Shrines. In this case it refers to a place where a ''kami'' is present.<ref name="IK">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑|qq=}} Japanese dictionary</ref> It can therefore be a shrine and, in fact, the characters 神社, 社 and 杜 can all be read "mori" ("grove").<ref name="BT43">Sonoda Minoru in ''Breen, Teeuwen'' (2000:43)</ref> This reading is present in the {{Lang|ja-latn|{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}|qq=}}.<ref name="BT43" /> This reading reflects the fact the first shrines were simply {{ill|Sacred grove|en|Sacred grove|qid=Q811600|lt=sacred groves|1=Sacred grove}} or [[Chinju no Mori]] where ''kami'' were present.<ref name="BT43" /> In modern Shinto, the [[shintai]] of shrine Shinto is understood to be the {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}, such as the main hall and {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}, which are covered with {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}, and the surrounding forests are understood to be the chinju-no-mori. In [[:en:Koshinto|Koshinto]], which is the original source of [[shinto]], there are [[Himorogi]], [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura]], forests, land covered with forests, mountains (such as {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}, etc.), megaliths, {{ill|Sea|en|Sea|qid=Q165|lt=seas|1=Sea}}, {{ill|River|en|River|qid=Q4022|lt=rivers|1=River}} (characteristic places such as {{ill|Reef|en|Reef|qid=Q184358|lt=Reef|1=Reef}}s and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|qid=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s), and {{ill|Nature|en|Nature|qid=Q7860|lt=Nature|1=Nature}} itself is an object of faith. In many shrine precincts and in the mountains, trees with distinctive trunks and branches and large trees are worshipped as [[Shinboku|sacred trees]]. Most Shinto shrines were originally built in such a Shinto shrine or on the border between the [[Tokoyo no kuni|everlasting world]] and the [[:en:Present world|Present world]], where there is a sacred stone or rock. In the same way as in the ancient Shinto religion, shrines like {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Omiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, which worships {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, the mountain itself is considered the [[Shintai]]. Some shrines do not even have a main shrine or a [[Honden]], and some use forests and {{ill|Hill|en|Hill|qid=Q54050|lt=Hill|1=Hill}}s as their sacred objects, thus preserving the ancient Shinto religion, which is also known as {{ill|Nature worship|en|Nature worship|qid=Q8189650|lt=Nature worship|1=Nature worship}} and {{ill|Animism|en|Animism|qid=Q43004|lt=spirit worship|1=Animism}} in Japan.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/2009dd21082302/http://www.munakata-taisha.or.jp/html/gyoji_kannabisai.html「宗像大社-公式ホームページ 神奈備祭について]</ref>。 According to {{ill|Akira Miyawaki|en|Akira Miyawaki|qid=Q418823|lt=Akira Miyawaki|1=Akira Miyawaki}}, "chinju no mori" is used as an academic term by the International Society of Vegetation Science. <ref>[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] {{ill|2009|en|2009|qid=Q1996|lt=2009|1=2009}}[[1月10日]] 12版6面 [[山田養蜂場]]の全面広告</ref><ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20160830172036/http://www.plantatree.gr.jp/academie/essay-miyawaki-akira-01.html 「木を植える-21世紀の鎮守の森を目指して-」] [[宮脇昭]]、{{ill|Nonprofit organization|en|Nonprofit organization|qid=Q163740|lt=NPO|1=Nonprofit organization}} PLANT A TREE PLANT LOVEサイト</ref>。 === Planted Chinju no Mori === {{See also|Afforestation in Japan|qq=}} Contrary to these examples, there are also examples of Chinju no Mori forests created for shrines via {{ill|Afforestation|en|Afforestation|qid=Q2384419|lt=Afforestation|1=Afforestation}}. The most famous example is [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]]. Some trees were brought in as donations from {{ill|Taiwan (island)|en|Taiwan (island)|qid=Q22502|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan (island)}} and other countries, but the basic policy is to give consideration to the vegetation ({{ill|Potential natural vegetation|en|Potential natural vegetation|qid=Q2106435|lt=Potential natural vegetation|1=Potential natural vegetation}}) that should originally exist in the area, and the forest was planned to look like a natural shrine forest through natural renewal in the future. The plan is designed to make the forest look like a natural township forest through natural renewal in the future. In addition, when [[Kasugayama Primeval Forest]] was registered as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, this point was taken into consideration and it was designated as a cultural heritage site instead of a natural heritage site. == Vegetation == It is believed that the township forests have been preserved as such since ancient times. Therefore, its forest vegetation is considered to have preserved the original {{ill|Vegetation|en|Vegetation|qid=Q187997|lt=Vegetation|1=Vegetation}} of the area, the so-called {{ill|Potential natural vegetation|en|Potential natural vegetation|qid=Q2106435|lt=original vegetation|1=Potential natural vegetation}}. Today, when the surrounding nature is often destroyed, township forests are often one of the few clues to the former nature of the area. For this reason, in {{ill|Forest ecology|en|Forest ecology|qid=Q2249329|lt=Forest ecology|1=Forest ecology}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the township forests are emphasized, called '''shrine forests''' or '''shrine and temple forests''', and are often the subject of surveys. In many cases, they are confirmed to be valuable in the process and are protected as {{ill|Natural monument|en|Natural monument|qid=Q23790|lt=Natural monument|1=Natural monument}}s or the like. In this sense, the forests associated with [[Utaki]] in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} are similar. However, it should not be assumed that the old vegetation has been completely preserved. If the surrounding area is developed and only the chinju forest remains in isolation, the originally wide and continuous vegetation will have been cut into small pieces. As a result, the area will become smaller, and it is very possible that some species will not be able to maintain their {{ill|Population|en|Population|qid=Q2625603|lt=Population|1=Population}} as a result. The topography of the area may not include areas along mountain streams, and many areas will be lost from the communities that existed in ancient times. This will also cause aridification and other changes. Some areas are changed by such changes, and some species are newly invaded. For example, {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|qid=Q158722|lt=camphora|1=Camphora officinarum}}, which is often seen in shrines, is not thought to have originally been found in the forests of central and southern Japan. Also, {{ill|Deciduous|en|Deciduous|qid=Q1131316|lt=deciduous trees|1=Deciduous}} such as {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|qid=Q920344|lt=Keyaki|1=Zelkova serrata}} and {{ill|Aphananthe aspera|en|Aphananthe aspera|qid=Q4779482|lt=Mucunoki|1=Aphananthe aspera}} should not occur much in the {{ill|Climax community|en|Climax community|qid=Q815521|lt=Climax community|1=Climax community}} south of the plains in central [[Honshu]]. Furthermore, in such fragmented vegetation, the {{ill|Animal|en|Animal|qid=Q729|lt=Animal|1=Animal}}s that live there often do not have enough room to move around to maintain their populations, and the animal communities are thought to be more degraded than the plant communities. Since many plants require specific animals for {{ill|Pollen|en|Pollen|qid=Q79932|lt=Pollen|1=Pollen}} vectoring, {{ill|Seed|en|Seed|qid=Q40763|lt=Seed|1=Seed}} dispersal, etc., it can be said that the degradation of animal communities is related to the transformation of the vegetation itself in various ways. Direct human disturbance also takes place. Often, non-wild plants are planted in the precincts. Also, when a tree falls in the forest, the gap created by the fall (in ecological terms {{ill|Gap dynamics|en|Gap dynamics|qid=Q11298166|lt=gap|1=Gap dynamics}}) is often filled by planting {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|qid=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}} or [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]]. There are reports of advanced use of forest resources in the precincts as early as the early modern period. <ref>鳴海邦匡・小林茂「近世以降の神社林の景観変化」歴史地理学48-1、{{ill|2006|en|2006|qid=Q2021|lt=2006|1=2006}}</ref>。In recent years, some forests have been undercut and raked for leaves, but this kind of maintenance is not necessary, and if done in natural forests, it can lead to increased devastation. In addition, forests are often cut off from their surroundings or mowed around them due to road expansions, etc. In such cases, the forests are often destroyed by clumps or mantle communities. In such cases, forests that have lost their mantle and sod communities are prone to drying out of the forest floor, which can lead to devastation. In addition, concrete spraying is increasingly used for restoration of these areas, which is an easy and inexpensive method that does not allow for natural recovery. Nevertheless, unlike so-called {{ill|Satoyama|en|Satoyama|qid=Q7426437|lt=Satoyama|1=Satoyama}}s, these forests are familiar to people, but they are treated differently from forests that are tended to for human use, and they continue to have a certain presence. == Decline == {{See also|Jinja gōshi|qq=}} In modern times, the belief in [[shinto]] deities has become more abstract, and the reverence for the dense forests of Shinto shrines has waned, with many believers, such as [[Ujigami|Ujiko]], not necessarily considering them necessary. While there are examples where the core shrine is so revered that the shrine forest is maintained (e.g., [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Jingu]] mentioned above, as well as [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu Forest]] in Kyoto City), there have been many cases where the shrine forest was cut down to make room for road repairs, agricultural and residential land development, and public facilities such as daycare centers. In particular, in urban areas, forests were often cut down. In some cases, especially in urban areas, forests have been completely lost. There are shrines that consist entirely of torii gates and main shrines, and the original meaning of shrines based on nature worship has been greatly diminished. As far back as before the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} era, there were shrines of various sizes in each village, and many of them had a forest for their Shinto shrine. This was greatly reduced by the so-called [[:en:Jinja gōshi|Jinja gōshi]] decree. As a result, many shrines were abolished, and at the same time, their guardian forests were cut down. {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|qid=Q6862791|lt=Minakata Kumagusu|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} has been opposed to [[:en:Shrine merging|Shrine merging]] since that time, fearing the large-scale destruction of nature that would result from this logging. [[:en:Jinja gōshi|Jinja gōshi]] was intended to incorporate indigenous beliefs into {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} by organizing only one shrine per administrative village, but one theory suggests that the purpose was to gain concessions on the {{ill|Lumber|en|Lumber|qid=Q1370714|lt=wood|1=Lumber}} resources or by-products ({{ill|Camphor|en|Camphor|qid=Q181559|lt=Camphor|1=Camphor}}, etc.) of this process. It is also said that the purpose was to acquire the wood resources or by-products (such as {{ill|Camphor|en|Camphor|qid=Q181559|lt=Camphor|1=Camphor}}). In particular, there are many large camphor trees and [[Machilus thunbergii|tabunoki]] in shrines in central Honshu and southward, and there is a theory that these trees may have been treated at a high price. On a more minor note, large plants of {{ill|Vanda falcata|en|Vanda falcata|qid=Q1037607|lt=furan|1=Vanda falcata}} and {{ill|Dendrobium moniliforme|en|Dendrobium moniliforme|qid=Q163663|lt=sekkok|1=Dendrobium moniliforme}}, which used to be common on sacred trees in shrines, are rarely seen anymore due to the wildflower boom. There was a similar case in the past, when {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|qid=Q6862791|lt=Minakata Kumagusu|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} found a large plant of a magnificent epiphytic orchid at a shrine in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, and happily explained it to the [[kannushi]], who promoted it to the fullest. The goddess was very pleased with it and explained it to him. The priest was very angry with him, saying, "How dare you advertise something so rare in such a way? It will be stolen." He was angry. == Revaluation and restoration == Following the conclusion of the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, widespread deforestation took place as part of national land development, separate from the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} religion. This led to increased reflection on the consequences of excessive development, {{ill|Rural flight|en|Rural flight|qid=Q2608153|lt=depopulation|1=Rural flight}}, and land shortage in the aftermath of the collapse of the bubble economy. This resulted in the rise of the {{ill|National trust|en|National trust|qid=Q1006283|lt=national trust movement|1=National trust}} and other initiatives aimed at preserving and restoring forests and green spaces. The {{ill|National trust|en|National trust|qid=Q1006283|lt=national trust movement|1=National trust}} has been successful in its efforts to conserve and rehabilitate forests and other vegetation. In the aftermath of the {{ill|2011_Tōhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami|en|2011_Tōhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami|qid=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}, there has also been a push to maintain and restore the forests affected by the disaster.<ref>[http://morinoproject.com/ 鎮守の森プロジェクト]（2018th year 5月19日閲覧）</ref><ref>[http://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/189651 鎮守の森を再び シャクナゲなど130本植樹 二里町大里地区住民]『[[佐賀新聞]]』LiVE（2018th year 3月7日）2018th year 5月19日閲覧</ref>。 == See also == * [[Chinjugami]] * {{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinjusha|1=Chinjusha}} * {{ill|Satoyama|en|Satoyama|qid=Q7426437|lt=Satoyama|1=Satoyama}} * {{ill|Green conservatism|en|Green conservatism|qid=Q1062758|lt=Green conservatism|1=Green conservatism}} * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Himorogi]] ** {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} * {{ill|Bai sema|en|Bai sema|qid=Q803695|lt=Bai sema|1=Bai sema}} * {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|qid=Q6862791|lt=Minakata Kumagusu|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} * {{ill|Potential natural vegetation|en|Potential natural vegetation|qid=Q2106435|lt=Potential natural vegetation|1=Potential natural vegetation}} * {{ill|Akira Miyawaki|en|Akira Miyawaki|qid=Q418823|lt=Akira Miyawaki|1=Akira Miyawaki}} == References == <references /> == External links == * [http://www2s.biglobe.ne.jp/~nippon/jogbd_h17/jog390.html 国柄探訪：「鎮守の森」を世界へ] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220518052950/http://www2s.biglobe.ne.jp/%257enippon/jogbd_h17/jog390.html |date=2022-05-18 |qq=}} {{Shinto shrine|qq=}}
[[en:Chinju no Mori]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:鎮守の森]] [[simple:Chinju no Mori]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11651158}} [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]]
